texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thin and oriented zeolite films are important for advanced separations, catalysis, and sensing. Strategies for tailoring zeolites for applications include controlling their crystal size, shape, and orientation. Here, three siliceous DDR zeolite ultrathin films with different orientations achieved by homoepitaxial growth from 60 nm-sized seed particles are reported. The 0.5 µ m thick membrane shows a separation selectivity of 400 for CO 2-CH4 mixtures and CO2 permeance of 25 × 10 -7  mol m -2 s -1  Pa -1 at 20 ° C and 1 bar, leading to a recordhigh performance among all reported DDR membranes. Furthermore, the seed nanoparticles are grown into mono-dispersed DDR sub-micron crystals with trigonal and tabular habits. These crystals are assembled in monolayers for the growth of ultrathin and uniformly (h0h)-oriented and c -oriented films with maximum surface pore diameter of 0.365 and 0.263 nm, respectively, by using the 1-adamantanamine template in fluoride medium. The novel strategy not only provides high-performance membrane candidates for industrial CO 2 separation, but also inspires interfacial engineering, pore size, and orientation controlling for other microporous crystals, and their membranes.

Here, three siliceous DDR zeolite ultrathin films with different orientations achieved by homoepitaxial growth from 60 nm-sized seed particles are reported. Furthermore, the seed nanoparticles are grown into mono-dispersed DDR sub-micron crystals with trigonal and tabular habits. These crystals are assembled in monolayers for the growth of ultrathin and uniformly (h0h)-oriented and c -oriented films with maximum surface pore diameter of 0.365 and 0.263 nm, respectively, by using the 1-adamantanamine template in fluoride medium. The novel strategy not only provides high-performance membrane candidates for industrial CO 2 separation, but also inspires interfacial engineering, pore size, and orientation controlling for other microporous crystals, and their membranes.

texts.orig: Zeolites display rigid microporous structures and are well known as catalysts, [1,2]  adsorbents, [3]  and continuous films or membranes serving  as  molecular  filters, [4,5] based  on  minute  differences  in pore shape or diameter. texts.orig: The  development  of  high-performance membranes for crucial gas separations such as CO2 capture, H 2  purification, and natural gas  purification  is  extremely  urgent  due  to the growing climate problems and resource shortages. [9-11] DDR  exhibited  high  separation efficiencies for CO2-N2,  CH4-N2, [12] propane-propene, [13] and olefin-paraffin mixtures. [14] The  8-MR  in  the  DDR  framework (pore dimension: 0.36 × 0.44 nm 2 ) can be  used  to  separate  CO 2 (kinetic  diameter of  0.33  nm)  from  either  N 2 (0.364  nm) [15] in, e.g., combustion exhaust [16] or CH4 (0.38  nm)  in,  e.g.,  natural/bio/shale  gas. [17] Molecular  simulations  have  indicated  that the Si-DDR displays the highest adsorption selectivity of 12 types of investigated zeolite frameworks  for  the  separation  of  CO 2 and CH4 [18] and has the capability to separate CO 2 from larger molecules also at humid condi- tions because of its hydrophobicity. [19]  Hydrophilic membranes are not as effective because of the preferred adsorption of H 2 O over CO2. Studies have shown that zeolite films synthesized using fluoride as mineralizing agent show even higher hydrophobicity than films synthesized using hydroxide as mineralizing agent, [20,21]  and have a lower density of lattice defects. [22] Considering that H 2 O is a ubiquitous component in industrial gas streams, a hydrophobic Si-DDR  film  grown  using  fluoride  as  mineralizing  agent  (i.e., Si-DDR-F)  is  desirable  for  the  separation  of,  e.g.,  CO 2 -N2  or CO2-CH4 in industrial applications.

texts.orig: However, randomly oriented zeolite films tend to crack during calcination, [23] and  in  contrast,  highly  oriented  zeolite  films  have been  shown  to  exhibit  superior  performance  compared  to  randomly  oriented  films. [24] Consequently,  a  recent  trend  in  zeolite membrane research is increased focus on controlling the orientation of ultrathin films formed by either 3D crystal monolayers [25-27] or  2D  nanosheets. [28,29]   Furthermore,  uniformly  oriented  membranes  may  improve  the  separation  performance  by  decreasing grain  boundary  defects [30] and  increasing  the  flux  by  shortening the diffusion path for certain orientations.

[33]  The kinetic diameters of He, NH3, H2 O, Ne, H 2 , CO2, N2, CO, and CH4 molecules are 0.26, 0.26, 0.265, 0.275, 0.289,  0.33,  0.364,  0.376,  and  0.38  nm,  respectively.  Consequently, only He, NH3, H2O, Ne, and H2 molecules should be able  to  permeate  the  6-MR.  Indeed,  molecular  dynamics  simulations  indicate  that  the  6-MR  of  a  clathrasil  cage  is  permeable  to  H 2 and  can  be  considered  a  prospective  candidate  for hydrogen  purification  and  storage. [34] Therefore,  selective  H 2 transport  over  larger  molecules,  across  the  6-MR  windows  in a  hydrophobic  zeolite  membrane  is  extremely  attractive. [35,36] Some 6-MR zeolite membranes have been reported including membranes comprising c -oriented MCM-22 (MWW type, with 0.26  nm  pore  size)  crystals  that  displayed  good  H 2 -separation performance, [37] and  SOD  (0.253  nm  pore  size)  crystals  that demonstrated good water separation ability. [38] However, ultrathin (0.5 µ m) and uniformly c -oriented DDR membranes with only 6-MR windows (0.263 nm pore size [39] ) exposed at the surface have not yet been reported in the open literature.

texts.orig: Efficient fabrication of thin and highly oriented DDR membranes is a challenge. The traditional method for the synthesis of  DDR  crystals  is  complicated  and  time  consuming, [40] and the produced crystals usually have a wide particle size distribution. This makes the assembly of a monolayer of uniform seed crystals  difficult.  Such  a  monolayer  is  essential  to  allow  epitaxial  growth of the seeds into thin and oriented membranes. Improved  strategies  for  the  synthesis  of  DDR  crystals  have been developed, which have decreased the synthesis period and simplified  the  fabrication  procedure. [41,42]   However,  if  1-ADA is used as a template, impurity phases such as DOH and SGT zeolite  may  form  as  by-products, [43]   and  the  synthesized  DDR membrane does not often exhibit high performance [40]  or preferred  orientation. [44] To  avoid  this  problem,  methyltropinium iodide  (MTI)  was  adopted  for  the  growth  of  a  preferentially (h0h)-oriented  DDR  membrane. [45]   However,  this  template  is not readily accessible as a consequence of the complicated synthesis of the molecule.

[45]   However,  this  template  is not readily accessible as a consequence of the complicated synthesis of the molecule.

texts.orig: In this study, we developed a novel strategy for the fabrication of ultrathin (350-800 nm) and hydrophobic (water contact angle ≈ 120 ° )  DDR  films  with  random  orientation,  uniform (h0h)-orientation and c -orientation through epitaxial growth of correspondingly  oriented  DDR  seed  monolayers  by  using  the readily available 1-ADA template in combination with fluoride as a mineralizing agent. First, parent DDR crystals ( ≈ 100 µ m) with well-developed trigonal shapes were synthesized [46,47]  and attrition  milled [48] to  nanocrystals  with  a  narrow  size  distribution.  In  the  next  step,  DDR  crystals  with  uniform  size  and habit were produced by adding the nanoparticles to appropriate synthesis mixtures followed by growth of the crystals by hydrothermal  treatment.  Figures  S2-S5  (Supporting  Information) illustrate crystals with the trigonal habit (1 µ m × 400 nm) and tabular habit (700 nm × 200 nm). texts.orig: 2.1. Synthesis and Separation Performance of Randomly Oriented DDR Thin Films
texts.text: 2.1. Synthesis and Separation Performance of Randomly Oriented DDR Thin Films

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  nanocrystals  prepared  by  milling  was  first  used  for  the preparation of monolayers and the growth of continuous thin films with random orientation. As shown in Figure 1 a, a glass surface coated with cationic polymer was seeded with a monolayer of DDR nanoparticles. [49]  The DDR film was grown in a synthesis gel with a molar composition of 1 SiO 2 :0.36 ADA:0.5 KF:200 H2O. The hydrothermal crystallization was conducted in an autoclave that was placed in an oven that was kept at a temperature of 150 ° C for 12 h. Characteristic trigonal zeolite DDR  crystals  were  observed  at  the  film  surface  (Figure  1b) while  the  view  of  the  cross-section  demonstrated  that  the  assynthesized  film  was  well  intergrown  and  with  thickness  of ≈ 0.5 µ m, as shown in Figure 1d. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern  reveals  reflections  from  the  (006),  (202),  (024),  (116), (018),  etc.,  planes  of  the  DDR  phase,  demonstrating  the random orientation of the crystals in the film (Figure S6, Supporting  Information).  Consequently,  this  film  displays  both 6-MR and 8-MR at the surface. It is worth mentioning that a 350 nm thick and randomly oriented DDR film can be formed after  6  h  of  growth,  and  prolonging  the  growth  time  to  24  h will increase the film thickness to 2 µ m (Figure S7, Supporting Information).  The  film  surface  is  quite  hydrophobic  with  a water contact angle of 120 ° (Insert of Figure 1b). This value is remarkably  higher  than  previously  reported  for  hydrophobic DDR films in the range 90-100 ° . [19,36]  Cationic templates, such as  methyltropinium  iodide [45] and N , N , N -trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide, [51]   with  the  use  of  hydroxide  as  mineralizing agent, induce breaking of the silicon-oxygen bond in order  to  balance  the  charge,  consequently,  -OH  groups  form after  calcination. [42]   Here,  the  higher  hydrophobicity  of  DDR films is attributed to the use of the non-ionic template 1-ADA, pure silica as building material for the framework and fluoride as mineralizing agent (Figure 1c).

texts.orig: The milled  DDR nanoparticles  were  adopted  as  seeds  for  the growth of well-shaped crystals, and the added seed amount was adjusted to a suitable level for synthesis of Si-DDR crystals with a  length  not  exceeding 1 µ m (Figure S8, Supporting Information).  The  monodispersed  Si-DDR  crystals  with  trigonal  habit were deposited onto supports via dynamic interfacial assembly with  the  aid  of  oleic  acid  as  surfactant,  resulting  in  the  formation  of  monolayers  of  close-packed  and  uniformly  (h0h)oriented crystals. Briefly, a cleaned substrate was immersed in a PE bottle containing DDI water, and a stirring bar was placed at the bottom of the bottle to keep the water surface in continuous rotation to serve as a dynamic interface. [50] Afterward, a stable zeolite  suspension,  which  was  produced  by  dispersing  DDR crystals with trigonal-habit in 2-butanol and a minute amount of  oleic  acid,  was  gently  injected  onto  the  dynamic  interface until a monolayer with full coverage formed. [61] Careful lifting of the substrate through the dynamic interface yielded a compact monolayer  of  uniformly  (h0h)-oriented  DDR  crystals  on  the substrate, as indicated by the SEM image shown in Figure 3 a. texts.orig: There was only one layer of crystals and no additional crystals could be observed on top of this layer. It is proposed that hydrophobic interactions between oleic acid molecules adsorbed on the surface of neighboring crystals aid the formation of highly close-packed  zeolite  monolayer.  XRD  data  showed  that  the crystals  were  uniformly  (h0h)-oriented,  as  only  (h0h)  reflections were observed in the diffraction pattern, as illustrated in Figure  3e.  The  XRD  pattern  recorded  for  the  powder  of  seed crystals shown in Figure 3e verified that the crystals with trigonal-habit  are  only  comprising  the  DDR  phase,  as  compared with a database pattern for pure DDR (Figure S2e, Supporting Information).

texts.orig: After  the  growth  of  the  crystals  in  the  monolayer  in  a  gel with molar composition of 1 SiO2:0.36 ADA:0.5 KF:200 H2O at 150 ° C for 6 h, a continuous DDR film without defects formed (Figure 3c). The crystals in the continuous DDR film maintained the preferential (h0h)-orientation, as only (h0h)-reflections were observed  in  the  XRD  pattern,  see  Figure  3e.  In  other  words, the  maximum  diameter  of  the  pores  displayed  at  the  surface is  0.365  nm  and  defined  by  8-MR  as  shown  in  Figure  3d.  In addition, the smaller pores defined by the 6-MR rings are also exposed  at  the  surface.  The  SEM  image  of  the  cross  section shown in Figure 3d illustrates that the thickness of the film is 800 nm, which can be compared to the 400 nm thick seed layer, see Figure 3b. DDR crystals with prism and octahedron shape were  also  synthesized  and  assembled  to  monolayers  of  (h0h)oriented crystals (Figures S8 and S9, Supporting Information).

texts.orig: Information).  Such  crystals  could  be  assembled  into  a  continuous, close-packed layer of (00l)-oriented crystals with large area  using  the  same  dynamic  interfacial  assembly  method (Figure  S11,  Supporting  Information).  The  SEM  image  of  the cross section shown in Figure 4b reveals that the layer is a monolayer without any crystals in a second layer. Note that nearly all DDR crystals are (00l)-oriented. The XRD data confirm that the crystals in the layer indeed are (00l)-oriented, (Figure 4e) as only the (003), (006), (009), (0012), (0015), (0018), and (0021) reflections are observed. In addition to glass plates, sheets of an aluminum alloy,  Au-coated  silicon  wafers,  macroporous  alumina discs, and hydrophobic PTFE discs were also used as substrates for assembly of monolayers of oriented DDR crystals (Figures  S12  and  S13,  Supporting  Information),  which  indicates the general applicability of this assembly approach. After growth of the crystals in the monolayer in a gel with the molar composition 1 SiO 2 :0.36 ADA:0.5 KF:0.28 KOH:200 H2O in an autoclave kept at 150 ° C for 10 h, a continuous and very smooth DDR  film  formed  (Figure  4c).  The  influence  of  the  synthesis time and the 1-ADA/SiO2 ratio on the growth of the seed layer were also studied (Figures S14 and S15, Supporting Information).

Such  crystals  could  be  assembled  into  a  continuous, close-packed layer of (00l)-oriented crystals with large area  using  the  same  dynamic  interfacial  assembly  method (Figure  S11,  Supporting  Information).  The  SEM  image  of  the cross section shown in Figure 4b reveals that the layer is a monolayer without any crystals in a second layer. Note that nearly all DDR crystals are (00l)-oriented. The XRD data confirm that the crystals in the layer indeed are (00l)-oriented, (Figure 4e) as only the (003), (006), (009), (0012), (0015), (0018), and (0021) reflections are observed. In addition to glass plates, sheets of an aluminum alloy,  Au-coated  silicon  wafers,  macroporous  alumina discs, and hydrophobic PTFE discs were also used as substrates for assembly of monolayers of oriented DDR crystals (Figures  S12  and  S13,  Supporting  Information),  which  indicates the general applicability of this assembly approach. After growth of the crystals in the monolayer in a gel with the molar composition 1 SiO 2 :0.36 ADA:0.5 KF:0.28 KOH:200 H2O in an autoclave kept at 150 ° C for 10 h, a continuous and very smooth DDR  film  formed  (Figure  4c).  The  influence  of  the  synthesis time and the 1-ADA/SiO2 ratio on the growth of the seed layer were also studied (Figures S14 and S15, Supporting Information).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Conclusion
texts.text: 3. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have developed a facile and economic route for the preparation  of  randomly  oriented,  uniformly  (h0h)-oriented,  and (00l)-oriented  DDR  films  with  submicrometric  thickness on a wide variety of substrates. The ultrathin and randomly oriented  membrane  possesses  exceptionally  high  CO2  permeance that is ≈ 10  times  improved.  For  uniformly  oriented films, the CPO values after optimized film synthesis are 1-2 orders  of  magnitude  higher  than  the  state-of-the-art  zeolite DDR  films.  Consequently,  the  oriented  DDR  films  exposed pores with a controllable and different maximum diameters at the surface, which is not only have great potential in CO 2 separation and H2 purification, but also desirable for use in various  fields  involving,  e.g.,  catalysis,  sensors,  or  optical devices.
